summary: Patrick McKenzie quantifies exactly how much customer support his micro-ISV (Bingo Card Creator) requires, finding it far less burdensome than skeptics assume. Over a six-week period with 600+ installations, 2,000 downloads, and 32 sales, he wrote only 27 emails total. He breaks down the emails by category and calculates his effective hourly rate for support at roughly $370/hour. He argues that even scaling to $100,000 in annual revenue would only mean about 11 emails per day, and suspects support scales sub-linearly with customers.
key_points: Customer support for a simple B2C software product required only 27 emails over six weeks across 600+ installations and 32 sales — about 0.84 emails per sale
The median support email takes 3 minutes to write, and judicious use of auto-text templates makes responses both faster and more consistent for customers
When multiple customers ask about the same issue, the right response is to fix the root cause (better documentation, clearer instructions) rather than just answering each email
Support burden likely scales sub-linearly with customers — closer to constant or logarithmic rather than linear
Customer expectations for support on a $24.95 product are inherently low, which works in the solo founder's favor
money_quotes: If you assume my average is about 4 minutes an email, which is pretty close to accurate, then I am paying myself roughly $370 an hour, give or take, to support Bingo Card Creator.
If it scaled linearly with customers and I was selling 5,000 units a year (income in excess of $100,000 USD) I'd be writing a backbreaking 11 emails a day.
I have strong doubts, incidentally, that support emails scale linearly with customers: my intuition says its actually closer to constant or perhaps logarithmic.

People aren't paying you money so that Direct Access can have a conversation with them. You need to read, understand, and resolve their issue rather than skimming, classifying, and auto-replying to their issue.
When I get multiple inquiries about a single subject I figure out how I can avoid getting them again.
Suffice it to say that no school district has ever contemplated a Service Level Agreement for their mission critical bingo card needs.
I follow the rule that the CS rep should always reply to every email.
key_insight: Customer support for a simple B2C software product is far less burdensome than most people assume, and the real leverage comes from systematically eliminating repeat inquiries rather than just answering them faster.
